quotes.text: Never knew a prospector yet that died rich.
quotes.speaker: The Treasure of the Sierra Madre (1948)
quotes.context: Grizzled old prospector Howard (Walter Huston) offering hard-won wisdom about the gold-hunting life.

source.title: The Treasure of the Sierra Madre - Wikiquote

quotes.speaker: Toy Story (1995)
quotes.context: Buzz Lightyear's heroic catchphrase as he strikes a pose and 'flies'

quotes.text: He's not a bad kid. He's just been told he's nobody for so long he started believing it.
quotes.speaker: Good Will Hunting (1997)
quotes.context: Sean Maguire explaining Will to Lambeau during one of their faculty arguments
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Sean's compassionate, plain-spoken defence of Will is central to Williams's performance, and this line fits his register. The subtle giveaway is that it leans slightly too close to a thesis statement about the film's theme rather than a reactive moment in conversation.

quotes.text: A million dollars isn't cool. You know what's cool? A billion dollars.
quotes.speaker: The Social Network (2010)
quotes.context: Sean Parker (Justin Timberlake) pitching ambition to Mark Zuckerberg and Eduardo Saverin at a restaurant; the punchline lands in an intercut deposition scene

quotes.text: You mess with the bull, sweetheart, you get the whole herd.
quotes.speaker: The Breakfast Club (1985)
quotes.context: Principal Vernon warning Claire in the hallway before detention begins
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Vernon's pompous, half-formed toughness is all over the film, and 'you mess with the bull' is literally a real Vernon line, so extending it this way feels native. The giveaway is 'the whole herd' is a clumsy malapropism even for Vernon; the real film keeps his bluster simpler.

quotes.text: Same time tomorrow, Mr. Wonka? — I'm afraid not, Charlie. Tomorrow we do something completely different.
quotes.speaker: Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory (1971)
quotes.context: Charlie and Wonka at the factory gates after an early tour day
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Wilder's Wonka pivots on a dime between warmth and cryptic deflection, and 'completely different' is perfectly Wonka-vague. The giveaway is the exchange is tidier than Wilder's actual non-sequitur delivery style; real Wonka lines tend to quote poetry or veer sideways.
